texts.full_text: s to a transmittal article or envelope for mailing a magnetic card. 2. Description of the Prior Art Recently, magnetic cards in the form of telephone charge cards have beenfrequently used in place ofcash. In addition, the value of themagnetic card as a present or a gift has been popularized, such that magneticcards are used variously as presents. In the case where these magnetic cards are sent as presents, a magnetic card is put into an envelope, or the corners of a magnetic cHere’s the completed text based on the context and logical continuation of the patent description: --- 2. Description of the Prior Art Recently, magnetic cards in the form of telephone charge cards have been frequently used in place of cash. In addition, the value of the magnetic card asa present or a gift has been popularized, such that magnetic cards areusedvariously as presents. In the case where these magnetic cards are sent as presents, a magnetic card is put into an envelope, or the corners of a magnetic card are folded or bent to secure it within theenvelope. However, such conventional methods often fail to adequately protect themagnetic strip of thecard from damage caused by bending,friction, orexposure during transit. Additionally, folded corners may loosen over time, risking the card’s displacement or loss within the envelope. These shortcomings highlight the need for an improved transmittal article or envelope specifically designed to securely and safely mail magnetic cards while preserving their functionality and aestheticpresentati

texts.full_text: the author was brought up — in this case, Islam. The author mentions Why I Am Not a Christian towards the end of the first chapter, stating that many of its arguments also apply to islam. Why I Am Not a Property Dualist, an essay by John Searle in which he criticises the philosophical position of property dualism. Why I Am Not a Scientist (2009) , by biological anthropologist Jonathan M. Marks Similar titles How I STopped Being a Jew, is a 2014 book by Israeli historian Shlomo Sand.

texts.full_text: several high-profile cases involving corruption and organized crime within Boston's city government. Later years After leaving office as District Attorney, Sughruce returned to private practice in Charlestown with a focus on civil litigation until the early 1920s. He then retired from law due to health reasons but remained active in local politics and civic affairs for several more years before his death at age of 74. Legacy Michael J.
